source_texts: “It was all thanks to Kamsa that I was able to enter into this city,” said Vandiya Devan and started recounting yet another adventure of his. Vandiya Devan knew that the city would be on alert and guards would be posted at the gates to capture him. They also knew that he would come bearing the Pazhuvur Signet ring so he couldn’t possibly use it to gain entry.

All six tiny little rooms were steam heated, as a result of which you were suffocated by moist heat in the apartment. The window panes sweated; the visitors sweated; the maid and the husband sweated; Sofia Petrovna Likhutina herself was covered with perspiration, like warm dew on a chrysanthemum. How could there possibly be any perspective?
The Prince produced the twenty-five-rouble note and held it out to Ferdyshchenko. He unfolded it, inspected it, then turned it over and brought it up to the light.
In the vestibule Nikolai Apollonovich stopped in front of the lackey, trying to catch a thought that had scampered off.
“That is not right, it’s a mistake!” Nina Alexandrovna intervened suddenly, looking almost ruefully at the Prince. “Mon mari se trompe.” *

He told them. But we shall not repeat it here. He told the story briefly and impersonally. He did not speak of his love and his feelings; he told them only that he was no longer so determined to shoot himself “in view of certain new developments.” He did not bother to explain the motives behind his actions anymore, did not go into subtleties. Besides, his interrogators did not interrupt him very often to ask him questions; it was obvious that this was not what interested them most.
“Sit down, Ivan, please, here on the sofa,” Alyosha said, frightened. “You’re feverish. Lie down. Put your head on the pillow. Right. Would you like a wet towel on your head? It might help.”

We started laughing and joking, we talked without stopping the whole time, I didn't know what I was saying, I was happy. She told me she had seen me one time earlier, a long time ago, in the theater. I was with three friends and had behaved like a lunatic; no question, I had been drunk that time too, that was naughty.
"What is there to say concerning love?" at length said Bazarov. "In passing, you mentioned a certain Madame Odintsov (I think that was the name?). Who is she?"

“Well, I’d rather put it this way: you and I, we have the same philosophy. That would be a much fairer statement. Je pense, donc je suis, that is something I know for certain. As for the rest—all those worlds, God, and even Satan himself—I’m not sure whether all that exists independently or is merely a passing and subjective emanation of myself .
rationales: Outputs 0 and 3 share nearly identical phrasing and structure: both open with 'I owe my entry into this city entirely to Kamsa,' both use 'launched into another/yet another tale of his exploits,' both use 'Pazhuvur Signet ring' with the same capitalization, both describe guards stationed 'specifically to apprehend him,' and both end with the theme of the ring being unusable for gaining passage. The formal tone and sentence constructions are virtually identical across both outputs.

Outputs 1 and 2 share very similar stylistic patterns: both use 'six small/cramped rooms were heated by steam, creating a stifling and humid' construction, both describe the chrysanthemum comparison as 'gentle dew/moisture on a chrysanthemum,' both use an interpretive concluding sentence about the impossibility of maintaining perspective or comfort, and both have a flowing explanatory style that softens the original text's imagery. These shared fingerprints strongly suggest they come from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 1 and 4 share the same stylistic fingerprint: both use 'twenty-five-rouble note' (British spelling), both separate the prince's act of producing the note from handing it over, and crucially, both describe the same four sequential actions (unfolded, looked at, turned over, held to light) in nearly identical phrasing and order. The sentence rhythm and word choices are very closely matched compared to the other outputs.

Outputs 0 and 3 share the same paraphraser's fingerprints: both add a descriptive adjective for the vestibule/entrance-hall ('sumptuous' vs 'magnificent'), both include 'old' before the servant ('old lackey' vs 'old servant'), both use the verb 'catch' for the thought, and both preserve the 'running away' metaphor for the thought ('had run away' vs 'escaping thought'). They also share the same sentence structure with a fronted location phrase. These parallel stylistic choices — adding descriptors not strictly necessary, consistent lexical choices — point to the same paraphraser.

Outputs 3 and 4 share multiple distinctive stylistic features: both capitalize 'Prince', both use the phrase 'somewhat regretful' (vs. 'ruefully', 'sadly', or 'anguish' in other outputs), both explicitly include 'My husband is mistaken' (rather than the French phrase or omitting it), and both follow a similar sentence structure with an action verb followed by a participial phrase describing the glance/expression toward the Prince.

Outputs 2 and 4 share highly distinctive overlapping phrases: 'briefly and impersonally,' 'did not go into subtleties,' 'certain new developments/circumstances,' 'interrogators did not interrupt him very often,' and both include the meta-narrative aside about not repeating the story (placed at the end in output 2, at the beginning in output 4). These specific lexical and structural fingerprints strongly indicate they came from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 1 and 3 share the same stylistic fingerprints: both use Russian-style em-dash punctuation after dialogue attribution, both employ fragmented imperative sentence structures, both use nearly identical phrasing ('fever/feverish', 'wet towel', 'lie down, put your head on the pillow'), and both have a somewhat literal, slightly awkward translation quality compared to the more polished outputs.

Outputs 1 and 3 share highly similar vocabulary and phrasing: both use 'laugh and joke/jest,' 'like a madman,' 'drunk that time too,' and nearly identical sentence constructions. The core word choices and stylistic tone (slightly formal, close to the source) are consistent between these two, suggesting they come from the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 0 and 4 share very specific phrasing choices: 'What is/what's there to say about love', 'By the way, you mentioned', and 'Madame Odintsov'. Output 4 appears to be an elaborated version of output 0's template, with added narrative framing but retaining identical key phrases. No other pair shares this combination of 'what's there to say about love' + 'by the way' + 'you mentioned'.

Outputs 2 and 3 share nearly identical structural patterns and phrasing. Both use 'more accurate way to put it,' both describe philosophies being 'aligned/align,' and both use the concept of 'fleeting/transient...manifestations of my own mind/being' for the same passage. Their sentence architecture mirrors each other closely, distinguishing them from the more casual (1), literal (4), or differently structured (0) outputs.

How could there be any perspectives? All six tiny rooms were heated by steam, which resulted in the apartment suffocating in moist heat. The window panes sweated; visitors sweated; the maid and the husband sweated; Sofya Petrovna Likhutina was covered with perspiration, like warm dew on a chrysanthemum.
The prince produced the twenty-five-rouble note and handed it to Ferdyshchenko. He unfolded it, looked it over, turned it over and held it up to the light.
Stefan found himself paused in the entrance hall, still grappling with a fleeting idea that had momentarily escaped his grasp, as he stood facing the footman waiting nearby.
"Nonsense," Nina Alexandrovna exclaimed unexpectedly, casting a somewhat regretful glance at the Prince. "My husband is mistaken."

He told them. But we shall not tell it here. He told them, briefly and impersonally; he did not talk about love and feelings; he only said that he was no longer so determined to shoot himself, in view of certain new developments; he did not bother to explain the reasons for his actions, he did not go into subtleties; besides, his interrogators did not interrupt him very often to ask him questions; it was evident that it was not this that interested them most.

"Alyosha urgently instructed Ivan to sit down on the sofa, his voice trembling with concern. Ivan's feverish condition was evident, and Alyosha was worried. He encouraged Ivan to recline and lift his head to rest on the pillow. To help Ivan's fever, Alyosha offered a wet towel to place on his head."

We began to laugh and joke, our conversation flowing endlessly. I was so joyful, I barely remember what I was saying. She then revealed that she had seen me once before, many years ago, at the theater. Apparently, I was with three friends and acting wildly; it sounds like I was probably drunk then too, which was quite mischievous of me.

Bazarov thought for a moment and then asked in a fairly casual tone, "What's there to say about love, anyway?" He steered the conversation towards a different topic, inquiring, "By the way, you mentioned someone named Madame Odintsov - correct me if I'm wrong? And who is this person?"

"Well, if you like, I have the same philosophy as you, that would be true. _Je pense, donc je suis_, I know that for a fact; all the rest, all these worlds, God and even Satan--all that is not proved, to my mind. Does all that exist of itself, or is it only an emanation of myself, a logical development of my ego which alone has existed for ever: but I make haste to stop, for I believe you will be jumping up to beat me directly."
